# Typed Events

## Description

This project aims to offer support for event driven code that takes good advantage of Typescript's type system.

## Example

### Event Class
If you want to communicate any information with the outside world through events, you create an `Event` object. This object follows closely with the API we are familiar with from the NodeJS `events.EventEmitter` class with methods like `on`, `once`, `removeListener`, `removeAllListeners` and `emit`.

Represents an event which can be made to emit data and others can subscribe to. Conforms to API of `events.EventEmitter` with the exception of the event name which is now not needed since each event represents a singular event, not a collection of events.

```js
import { Event } from 'jibo-typed-events';

// We create an event which will optionally accept numeric data
let ev1 = new Event<number>('Event1');

// Works great and type of num is known by IDE
ev1.on( (num) => console.log('Number is: ' + num) );

// Doesn't compile as 'indexOf' is not a method on number
ev1.once( (num) => console.log('Error: ' + num.indexOf('c')) );

ev1.emit(37); // Works
ev1.emit('error'); // Doesn't compile

```

### EventContainer Class

Here is a useful pattern to follow for classes that interact with their environment heavily through events. We can create a public `events` object which lists out descriptively named events, each of a different type. 

This makes it significantly easier to track where outside code is connecting to our events and is especially useful for refactoring. 

```js
import { Event, EventContainer } from 'jibo-typed-events';

// Define a person datatype
interface Person { name: string; age: number; }


// Define our event interface with some descriptively named events
class SomeEvents extends EventContainer {
	
    incomingMessage: new Event<string>('Incoming message'),
    personArrived: new Event<Person>('Person has arrived'),

	constructor() {
		super();
	}
}


// The events can then be used by a class defining its event API clearly
class SomeClass {
  // The member `events` becomes the namespace of all events that SomeClass offers
  //   For complex event interfaces we could create a two layer eventcontainer 
  //   namespace (ex: `events.out.msgRcvd`)
  events = new SomeEvents();

  someMethod() {
    // Here (or anywhere else) we might emit some events
    this.events.incomingMessage.emit('Some string typed message content');
    this.events.personArrived.emit({ name: 'George', age: 37 });
  }
}


let s = new SomeClass();
// Now we have static access to typed events available on a SomeClass instance
s.events.incomingMessage.on( (msg) => console.log('Message: ' + msg) );
s.events.personArrived.on( (person) => console.log('Age: ' + person.age) );

// EventContainer also contains an old-school NodeJS EventEmitter
// to use when you need to generate dynamic events
s.events.untyped.on('SomeEventName', (data) => console.log(data) ); 
s.events.untyped.emit('SomeEventName', 42); 

// Lastly we can clear all events since SomeEvents is an `EventContainer`
s.events.removeAllListeners();

```
